ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for shioyaki-style grilled sea bass set meal; closest match is sea bass food product (FOODON_03315091). Set meal composite not individually coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers grilled sea bass as a component; no composite set-meal code exists. Used as primary anchor for the fish component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT code for shioyaki grilled sea bass set meal as a composite dish.

allergens: gluten (miso paste may contain barley or wheat)

serving.description: 1 set meal (grilled sea bass, steamed rice, miso soup, side dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal portion of approximately 650 g, comprising a grilled sea bass fillet, a bowl of steamed white rice, a bowl of miso soup with tofu and seaweed, and small side dishes such as pickles or salad.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Composite estimate: Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175159); White rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756); Miso soup with tofu (FDC ID 172453); adapted for shioyaki set meal proportions
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for a shioyaki grilled sea bass set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by component-weighted composition: approximately 30% grilled sea bass (USDA FDC 175159), 45% cooked white rice (USDA FDC 169756), 20% miso soup with tofu (USDA FDC 172453), and 5% pickled/salad side dishes. Input per_100g values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA composite estimate and found broadly consistent. Values retained from input with minor adjustments to align with USDA-derived composite. Sodium of 276.9 mg/100g is plausible given miso paste and salt-grilling contributions.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. White rice, boiled, GI 64 (glucose reference); University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice used as carbohydrate-weighted composite anchor.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Diabetes Association (Diabetes Care journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a shioyaki grilled sea bass set meal. GI estimated using carbohydrate-weighted method: the dominant carbohydrate source is steamed white rice (GI 64, University of Sydney GI Database, glucose reference). Sea bass and miso soup contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially alter the composite GI. Composite GI of 64 assigned as a medium-GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 64 × 65 g carbohydrate in 650 g serving at 10 g/100g) / 100 = 41.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 276.9 mg/100g (approximately 1,800 mg per 650 g set meal), primarily from miso paste, soy sauce, and salt-grilling technique
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced miso paste and minimal salt during grilling, avoid adding soy sauce at the table, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake toward the recommended 2,000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content at 10 g/100g (approximately 65 g per serving) from high-GI white rice, partially offset by protein (6.9 g/100g) and low sugar (0.77 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice or a reduced rice portion to lower the glycaemic load, and pair the meal with the protein-rich sea bass component to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium is elevated at approximately 1,800 mg per serving; however, saturated fat is low at 0.92 g/100g and sea bass provides lean protein with some omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should prioritise the sea bass and vegetable components, request reduced miso and soy sauce to limit sodium, and note that the low saturated fat profile is favourable for lipid management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sea bass provides lean protein with omega-3 content; tofu contributes soy isoflavones with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is very low at 0.92 g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory components of this meal but should request reduced miso paste and soy sauce to minimise sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shioyaki Grilled Sea Bass Set Meal is a Japanese-style set meal (teishoku) served in Singapore, featuring a sea bass fillet prepared using the shioyaki technique — a traditional Japanese salt-grilling method in which the fish is lightly coated with salt and grilled over direct heat until the skin is crisp and the flesh is moist and flaky. The set typically includes a bowl of steamed white rice, a bowl of miso soup made with dashi stock, tofu, and wakame seaweed, and small side dishes such as pickled vegetables (tsukemono) or a simple salad. The flavour profile is clean, savoury, and lightly smoky, with the natural sweetness of the sea bass highlighted by the salt crust. Nutritionally, the set meal provides approximately 100 kcal per 100 g, with 6.9 g protein, 3.85 g fat (of which only 0.92 g is saturated), and 10 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Sodium is elevated at 276.9 mg/100g due to miso paste and salt-grilling, making this a moderate-to-high sodium meal at approximately 1,800 mg per full 650 g serving. The glycaemic index is estimated at 64 (medium) driven by the white rice component. Clinically, this meal is suitable for general healthy eating and rheumatoid arthritis management due to its low saturated fat and anti-inflammatory fish protein, but requires sodium modification for hypertension and cardiovascular disease, and rice portion control for type 2 diabetes management.

confidence_reason: No single approved source provides nutrient data for a shioyaki grilled sea bass set meal as a composite dish. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component entries (grilled sea bass FDC 175159, cooked white rice FDC 169756, miso soup with tofu FDC 172453) weighted by approximate portion proportions, and cross-checked against the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7 from GrabFood). The composite estimate is internally consistent and nutritionally plausible, but cannot be verified against a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite set meal is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as a single validated composite entry; component-level USDA data for sea bass does contain fatty acid data but reliable weighted composite values cannot be confidently derived without exact portion weights. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite set meal is not available as a single entry in approved sources. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite set meal is not available as a single entry in approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite set meal is not available as a validated single-entry composite in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. Nutrient source: USDA FoodData Central composite estimate from FDC IDs 175159 (sea bass, grilled), 169756 (white rice, cooked), 172453 (miso soup with tofu). Input data from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-checked and found broadly consistent. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists. Halal status not confirmed — miso paste and soy sauce brands used by the restaurant are unknown. Multilingual local names added in iteration 3 to satisfy G6: chinese_simplified (盐烤鲈鱼套餐), chinese_traditional (鹽烤鱸魚套餐), malay (Set Ikan Siakap Bakar Garam), tamil (உப்பு வறுத்த கடல் பாஸ் தொகுப்பு உணவு), hindi (नमक-ग्रिल्ड सी बास सेट मील). Although this dish is Japanese cuisine, it is served in Singapore and the multilingual names reflect the Singapore food-labelling context.

Iteration 3: Updated gi_source title and organization to explicitly cite University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) to satisfy G2 approved-source compliance for GI.
Iteration 3: Corrected japanese local_names entry from 塩焼き鯛定食 (tai = sea bream) to 塩焼き鱸定食 (suzuki = sea bass) for accuracy.
Iteration 3: Updated aliases_by_language japanese entry to match corrected Japanese script 塩焼き鱸定食 and updated flat aliases array accordingly.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1-Q8 scorecard. Primary fixes in iteration 3: (1) G6 multilingual naming compliance satisfied by adding Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names reflecting Singapore serving context; (2) G2 approved-source compliance for GI source strengthened by explicitly citing University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. 2008; (3) Q8 aliases consistency rule satisfied by adding all multilingual strings to both flat aliases array and aliases_by_language structured object; (4) Japanese script corrected from sea bream (鯛) to sea bass (鱸). Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central composite estimate — approved source. Ontology uses real defensible codes with honest closest_parent declarations. All three required health conditions present with specific clinical guidance.
